title: File streams and binary/text modes

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A file is opened for reading, fgets results are checked in a loop, feof and ferror distinguish completion from failure, and fclose is checked. Your goal is to implement text file persistence that handles open, parse, I/O and close failures. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Pointers, arrays and arithmetic; Structures; Validated text input; Object lifetime and ownership. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Language rule and operational model
sections.body: C streams represent formatted or binary I/O state; code opens with an intentional mode, checks every operation, distinguishes EOF from error, and closes owned streams. This rule is applied to concrete C source and observable behavior, not treated as a vocabulary definition alone.

sections.heading: Boundary and common failure
sections.body: Text translation, buffering, seek support, update-stream direction changes, encoding, and filesystem errors vary by implementation and stream kind. Therefore the draft explicitly rejects this beginner failure model: A null result from a read always means normal end-of-file and does not require checking stream error state.

includes: Open, read, write, check and close streams with clear ownership.
Implement text file persistence that handles open, parse, I/O and close failures.
excludes: Text translation, buffering, seek support, update-stream direction changes, encoding, and filesystem errors vary by implementation and stream kind.

outcomes: Implement text file persistence that handles open, parse, I/O and close failures.

examples.title: File streams and binary/text modes: defined case
examples.situation: A file is opened for reading, fgets results are checked in a loop, feof and ferror distinguish completion from failure, and fclose is checked.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the concrete source or state: A file is opened for reading, fgets results are checked in a loop, feof and ferror distinguish completion from failure, and fclose is checked.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the conclusion: After a read stops, distinguish EOF from error with the stream state and validate the number of elements actually transferred.
examples.result: The defined result follows because After a read stops, distinguish EOF from error with the stream state and validate the number of elements actually transferred.

examples.title: File streams and binary/text modes: boundary case
examples.situation: Binary data uses fread element counts and handles a short read rather than assuming the requested byte count always arrives.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Identify the changed source or state: Binary data uses fread element counts and handles a short read rather than assuming the requested byte count always arrives.
Step 2 — Keep this standard boundary visible: Text translation, buffering, seek support, update-stream direction changes, encoding, and filesystem errors vary by implementation and stream kind.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting rule: A null result from a read always means normal end-of-file and does not require checking stream error state.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Implement text file persistence that handles open, parse, I/O and close failures.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: A null result from a read always means normal end-of-file and does not require checking stream error state.
misconceptions.correction: After a read stops, distinguish EOF from error with the stream state and validate the number of elements actually transferred.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Using concrete C rules, explain why this belief fails: A null result from a read always means normal end-of-file and does not require checking stream error state.

analogy.example: A manually managed workshop can model File streams and binary/text modes: every tool, address, buffer, and acquired resource has an explicit valid range and owner.
usefulFor: Making low-level state, bounds, representation, ownership, and cleanup responsibilities concrete
limitations: C abstract-machine rules, undefined behavior, pointer provenance, translation, and implementation variation are stricter than a physical workshop analogy.
Text translation, buffering, seek support, update-stream direction changes, encoding, and filesystem errors vary by implementation and stream kind.
